bytes.topic: GraphQL — schema design, resolvers, N+1 with DataLoader, subscriptions
bytes.title: GraphQL DataLoader — Batching Resolvers to Kill the N+1 Problem
bytes.body: Every GraphQL list resolver hides an N+1 trap. Query `posts { author { name } }` with a naive resolver and you'll fire one SQL query per post — 100 posts means 101 queries. DataLoader fixes this by batching all `author` loads within a single tick of the event loop into one `WHERE id = ANY($1)` call. Critically, DataLoader is request-scoped — instantiate it per request inside your context factory, never as a singleton. Each DataLoader takes a batch function that receives an array of keys and must return a same-length, same-order array of values (or Errors). The built-in per-request cache means repeated loads of the same key within a request are deduplicated automatically. For subscriptions, DataLoader doesn't apply the same way — subscription resolvers push events, not pull data, so batch your DB reads before publishing to the PubSub topic. Schema design matters too: expose connection types (`PostConnection`) over plain arrays to make pagination cursor-based, keeping resolver logic predictable and DataLoader batches stable across pages.

bytes.topic: WebSockets — connection lifecycle, heartbeat, reconnection, rooms
bytes.title: GraphQL WebSocket Subscriptions — Connection Lifecycle, Heartbeat, and Reconnection with graphql-ws
bytes.body:
GraphQL subscriptions over WebSocket use the `graphql-ws` protocol (not the deprecated `subscriptions-transport-ws`). The lifecycle begins with a `ConnectionInit` message from the client; the server responds with `ConnectionAck` before any `Subscribe` messages are accepted. Each active subscription gets a unique `id` — the server streams `Next` payloads and terminates with `Complete`.

For heartbeats, `graphql-ws` has built-in `Ping`/`Pong` frames. Set `keepAlive` on the server to emit pings at an interval; clients that miss a pong are disconnected, preventing ghost connections.

Reconnection must be handled client-side. The `graphql-ws` `createClient` accepts a `retryAttempts` and `retryWait` function — use exponential backoff here. On reconnect, all active subscriptions are automatically re-sent because `createClient` replays pending `Subscribe` messages.

For rooms (topic isolation), model them as subscription arguments filtered server-side via `withFilter` from `graphql-subscriptions`. The WebSocket connection itself is shared; subscription `id`s act as logical room membership. Never use separate WebSocket connections per room — that defeats multiplexing.

bytes.topic: tRPC — end-to-end type safety, procedure types, React Query integration
bytes.title: GraphQL Developer's Guide to tRPC — Type Safety Without the SDL
bytes.body:
tRPC delivers end-to-end type safety without GraphQL's SDL, resolvers, or codegen pipeline. Where you'd define types in SDL and run GraphQL Code Generator to sync them to TypeScript, tRPC infers client types directly from server-side procedure definitions — zero generation step.

Procedures map cleanly to GraphQL operations: `query` for reads, `mutation` for writes, `subscription` for streaming. Each procedure uses Zod for input validation — analogous to GraphQL input types — and the return type is inferred automatically. No `__typename` juggling, no fragment management, no `DocumentNode` identity issues.

React Query integration via `@trpc/react-query` mirrors Apollo Client's `useQuery`/`useMutation` API surface. `trpc.getUser.useQuery({ id })` generates a fully typed React Query hook — cache keys are derived automatically from the procedure name and input, unlike Apollo where you manage document identity manually.

The tradeoff is real: tRPC is TypeScript-only and monorepo-bound, while GraphQL is language-agnostic and excels at federated APIs across teams. If your stack is Next.js + TypeScript with no external API consumers, tRPC eliminates the SDL-to-type sync overhead entirely.

bytes.topic: REST — resource naming, HTTP verbs, status codes, idempotency
bytes.title: GraphQL's REST Blind Spots — Status Codes, Idempotency, and the Single Endpoint
bytes.body:
GraphQL abandons REST's per-resource URL structure for a single POST /graphql endpoint, quietly breaking several HTTP conventions. The most dangerous: status codes. A GraphQL response is almost always 200 OK — even when a resolver throws. Errors live in the response body's `errors` array, not the status code. Any middleware or monitoring that gates on non-2xx will silently pass through application-level failures.

Idempotency shifts too. GraphQL queries are naturally idempotent — same query + same variables = same result, safe to retry. Mutations are not, unless explicitly designed as such (e.g., upserts keyed on a stable `id`). There's no HTTP verb to signal this distinction; the `query` vs `mutation` keyword is your only contract, and clients must respect it.

One place REST semantics resurface meaningfully: persisted queries. Apollo's Automatic Persisted Queries (APQ) and Relay's `@persistQueryString` allow read operations to be sent via HTTP GET, restoring CDN cacheability for query-heavy traffic. Without this, every GraphQL read is a POST — uncacheable by default at the network layer.

bytes.topic: API Versioning — URL vs header versioning, backward compatibility strategy
bytes.title: GraphQL API Versioning — Why You Probably Don't Need /v2
bytes.body:
GraphQL's type system is designed for evolution, not versioning. URL versioning (/v1/graphql, /v2/graphql) forces clients to migrate entire schemas at once — the opposite of GraphQL's additive model. Header versioning (X-Api-Version: 2) is marginally better but still creates schema forks you must maintain in parallel.

The idiomatic path is schema evolution with @deprecated. Add new fields freely — GraphQL is additive by default. Mark old fields deprecated with a reason, give clients a migration window, then remove them in a future release. Introspection lets clients detect deprecated fields programmatically.

For genuinely breaking changes — renaming a type, changing an argument from optional to required, altering a scalar — use a field alias pattern: keep the old field, introduce the new one with a clearer name, deprecate the old. If you truly need hard versioning (e.g. a public API with SLA guarantees), run two separate schema endpoints rather than multiplexing version logic inside a single resolver graph. Tools like GraphQL Inspector can diff schemas and flag breaking changes in CI before they reach production.

bytes.topic: gRPC — protobuf schemas, streaming types, when to use over REST
bytes.title: GraphQL vs gRPC — Protobuf Schemas, Streaming Types, and When to Drop REST Entirely
bytes.body:
GraphQL's SDL and gRPC's `.proto` files solve the same core problem — contract-first, typed interfaces — but gRPC operates below the HTTP application layer using binary Protocol Buffer serialization instead of JSON. Where GraphQL gives clients a single flexible endpoint, gRPC defines strongly-typed RPC methods with generated client stubs.

gRPC supports four streaming patterns that map directly to concepts GraphQL developers know: unary (identical to a query/mutation round-trip), server streaming (like a subscription, but over HTTP/2 multiplexing instead of WebSockets), client streaming, and bidirectional streaming. gRPC streams carry significantly lower overhead because there's no subscription resolver, no WebSocket handshake, and no JSON parse cost.

The practical split: keep GraphQL at the BFF/gateway layer where flexible client queries matter. Push gRPC into internal service-to-service communication — embedding pipelines, ranking services, or any call hitting 5k+ RPS where JSON serialization overhead compounds. In most GraphQL architectures, resolvers end up calling gRPC backends anyway. Modeling that boundary explicitly avoids the anti-pattern of REST hops between internal services that already share a schema contract.

bytes.topic: Authentication — OAuth2, JWT in headers, API keys, mTLS
bytes.title: GraphQL Authentication — JWT, API Keys, and Context Propagation
bytes.body:
GraphQL has no built-in auth layer — authentication lives outside the schema, in your server's context factory. Every resolver receives a `context` object built per-request, making it the canonical place to validate credentials and attach the caller's identity.

For JWT (OAuth2 Bearer), extract and verify the token in the context builder before any resolver runs. If verification fails, set `user: null` — don't throw yet. Let resolvers or a schema directive like `@authenticated` decide the response. This keeps auth logic centralized and testable independently of business logic.

For API keys, the same pattern applies: read `x-api-key` from headers in context, look up the key, and attach the associated principal.

mTLS is handled at the transport layer (reverse proxy or server TLS config), but the client certificate's subject can be forwarded via a trusted header (`x-client-cert-dn`) and consumed in the same context pipeline.

Never do auth inside individual resolvers — it creates inconsistent enforcement. Use a field middleware or schema directive to declaratively protect types and fields uniformly.

bytes.topic: OpenAPI — spec-first vs code-first, generating clients, documentation
bytes.title: GraphQL SDL-First vs Code-First — Picking Your Schema Strategy
bytes.body:
GraphQL's equivalent of OpenAPI's spec-first vs code-first debate is SDL-first vs code-first schema design. SDL-first means your `.graphql` files are the source of truth — resolvers are typed against them via tooling like `graphql-codegen`. Code-first (Nexus, TypeGraphQL, Pothos) derives the SDL from your TypeScript resolver definitions at runtime, so the schema is a build artifact, not a committed file.

SDL-first wins on contract stability: the schema is a versioned artifact any team can diff in PRs, and `@graphql-codegen/client-preset` reads it alongside your operation documents to emit fully-typed React hooks for Apollo Client or urql — zero hand-written types. Code-first wins on refactoring ergonomics: rename a field in TypeScript and the schema updates automatically, eliminating dual-maintenance drift.

Documentation is intrinsic to GraphQL. Any field annotated with a triple-quoted `"""` description is surfaced automatically in GraphiQL, Apollo Studio, and Altair — no separate OpenAPI YAML to maintain. For public or multi-client APIs, SDL-first is the safer default. For backend-internal schemas that churn frequently, code-first reduces overhead.

bytes.topic: Rate Limiting — per endpoint, per user, sliding window vs token bucket
bytes.title: GraphQL Rate Limiting — Per-Field Sliding Windows vs Token Buckets
bytes.body:
GraphQL's flexible query structure makes naive REST-style rate limiting ineffective. A single POST to `/graphql` can trigger 1 or 100 resolver calls — limiting by HTTP request count is meaningless.

For per-field limiting, attach rate limit logic inside individual resolvers or via a schema directive like `@rateLimit`. Libraries such as `graphql-rate-limit` let you configure limits per field per user using a sliding window: each request logs a timestamp, and the count of requests within the last N seconds is checked against a threshold. Sliding windows smooth out burst behavior but require storing per-user timestamp arrays in Redis.

Token bucket suits bursty-but-bounded traffic better. Each user starts with N tokens; each resolver invocation costs tokens; tokens replenish at a fixed rate. This allows short bursts while enforcing long-run averages — ideal for clients running complex nested queries.

For per-user isolation, key your store on `context.user.id + fieldName`. With `graphql-shield`, compose rate limit rules directly into your permission layer so they run before resolvers execute, avoiding wasted compute on blocked requests.

bytes.topic: Filtering & Sorting — query param conventions, GraphQL args, protobuf fields
bytes.title: GraphQL Filtering & Sorting — Designing Args That Scale
bytes.body:
GraphQL has no built-in filtering or sorting conventions, so the community has converged on structured input types rather than ad-hoc scalar arguments. The Relay spec popularized `first`/`after` for pagination, but for filtering and sorting you should define dedicated input types: a `FilterInput` for predicates and an `OrderByInput` for sort fields plus direction.

Avoid flat args like `nameContains: String, sortBy: String, sortDir: String`. They don't compose and break as fields grow. Instead, define `filter: UserFilterInput` and `orderBy: [UserOrderByInput!]`. Using a list for `orderBy` naturally supports multi-column sorting.

On the resolver side, map these input types to your ORM or SQL builder — never interpolate them directly into raw queries. Validate enum values at the GraphQL schema layer using `enum SortDirection { ASC DESC }` so invalid inputs are rejected before they hit your data layer.

This pattern keeps your schema self-documenting: introspection reveals exactly which fields are filterable and sortable without external API docs.

bytes.topic: Pagination — cursor vs offset, total counts, infinite scroll patterns
bytes.title: GraphQL Pagination — Cursor vs Offset, Total Counts, and Infinite Scroll
bytes.body:
Offset pagination (`first: 10, offset: 20`) is simple but breaks under concurrent writes — a new row inserted mid-scroll shifts every subsequent page. Cursor-based pagination solves this. The Relay Connection spec is the de facto standard: `edges[].cursor` is an opaque, base64-encoded pointer (usually a row ID or timestamp), and `pageInfo` exposes `hasNextPage` / `endCursor`. Pass `after: $endCursor` on the next fetch to resume exactly where you left off.

For infinite scroll, store `endCursor` in component state and merge incoming `edges` into the existing list. Apollo Client's `fetchMore` handles this cleanly — use a `merge` field policy on the type to concatenate edge arrays rather than replace them.

Avoid `totalCount` unless the UI explicitly needs it. It forces a `COUNT(*)` subquery on every request, which is expensive on large tables. If you need it, request it only on the first page fetch via a separate field or a `@skip(if: $skipCount)` directive. Never include it in subsequent `fetchMore` calls.

bytes.topic: Security — input validation, CSRF, injection, GraphQL introspection risks
bytes.title: GraphQL Security — Locking Down Introspection, Injection, and CSRF
bytes.body:
GraphQL's flexibility is also its attack surface. Three threats demand immediate attention.

**Introspection exposure** hands attackers your entire schema. Disable it in production via `NoSchemaIntrospectionCustomRule` from `graphql-validation-depth` or Apollo Server's `introspection: false`. Never rely on security-through-obscurity — also set `allowBatchedHttpRequests: false` to block query batching abuse.

**Injection** hits through unsanitized arguments passed to resolvers. Never interpolate GraphQL variables directly into raw SQL or shell commands. Use parameterized queries via pg or Prisma — the variable `$userId` in a query must flow into `WHERE id = $1`, not string concatenation.

**CSRF** is real even with JSON bodies if your server accepts `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` or `text/plain`. Enforce `Content-Type: application/json` on every mutation and validate the `Origin` header server-side. Apollo Server 4 does this by default via CSRF prevention middleware.

**Depth and complexity limits** prevent DoS via nested queries. Use `graphql-depth-limit` and `graphql-query-complexity` to cap both. Set `maxDepth: 7` and a complexity threshold before queries hit resolvers.

bytes.topic: Testing — contract testing, mocking, integration tests per protocol
bytes.title: GraphQL Contract Testing — Lock Down Your Schema with MSW and graphql-codegen
bytes.body:
GraphQL's typed schema is a contract. Break it silently and every client breaks with it. Contract testing enforces that contract before a breaking change ships.

Use `graphql-codegen` to generate TypeScript types from your SDL, then drive your MSW mock server with those exact types. Any resolver returning a shape that doesn't satisfy the generated type fails at compile time — before a single integration test runs.

For integration tests, skip REST-style HTTP interceptors. Use `graphql-request` or Apollo Client's `MockedProvider` directly against a real in-memory `graphql` execution engine built with `makeExecutableSchema` from `@graphql-tools/schema`. This tests the full resolver chain — arguments, context injection, error propagation — without a network hop.

For mutation contract tests, assert on the exact `errors` array shape, not just `data`. GraphQL returns HTTP 200 even on partial failures; your test suite must check `data === null` and `errors[0].extensions.code` explicitly. Use `graphql-tag` to parse queries at test time so any syntax error surfaces immediately, not at runtime.

bytes.topic: Idempotency — safe vs idempotent methods, idempotency keys for mutations
bytes.title: GraphQL Idempotency Keys — Making Mutations Safe to Retry
bytes.body:
GraphQL queries are both safe (no side effects) and idempotent — repeating them never changes server state. Mutations are neither. A retried `createOrder` mutation can create duplicate orders, charge customers twice, or corrupt state if the network hiccups mid-flight.

The fix: add an `idempotencyKey: ID!` argument to sensitive mutations. The resolver checks a cache (Redis or a DB table) — if the key already exists, it returns the stored result instead of re-executing. Keys are client-generated UUIDs tied to the logical operation, not the request.

Apollo Server has no built-in idempotency middleware, so you implement this in the resolver or a shared util. Store `(key, result, expiresAt)` — a 24-hour TTL is typical for payment flows. The response shape must be identical on replay, so cache the full resolver return value, not just a status flag.

Critically, `idempotencyKey` should be defined on the mutation input type, not as a standalone argument, so it travels cleanly through fragments and generated client types.

bytes.topic: Caching — HTTP cache headers, CDN, ETag and conditional requests
bytes.title: GraphQL HTTP Caching — APQ, Cache-Control, and ETags Without Breaking POST Semantics
bytes.body:
GraphQL's single POST `/graphql` endpoint is a CDN dead zone — HTTP caches don't cache POST responses by definition. The escape hatch: send read operations as GET requests via Automatic Persisted Queries (APQ). The client hashes the query with SHA-256 and sends only the hash as a GET parameter. On a miss, the server returns `PersistedQueryNotFound`; the client retries with the full query body, the server registers the hash, and future requests use the short cacheable URL.

Apollo Server's `@cacheControl` directive lets you annotate fields individually. The plugin computes the minimum `maxAge` across all resolved fields in the response tree and writes a single `Cache-Control` header — so one uncacheable field (`maxAge: 0`) poisons the whole response. Mark user-specific fields `scope: PRIVATE` to block CDN storage while still allowing browser caching.

For ETags, `@apollo/server-plugin-response-cache` hashes the full response and emits an `ETag` header. On repeated GET requests with `If-None-Match`, the plugin short-circuits resolver execution entirely and returns `304 Not Modified` — no DB round-trip, no resolver overhead.

bytes.topic: Error Handling — consistent error format across protocols, problem+json
bytes.title: GraphQL Error Extensions — Enforcing a problem+json Contract in Every Response
bytes.body: GraphQL's `errors` array is spec-defined, but `extensions` is your escape hatch for structured metadata. Instead of leaking raw messages or scattering ad-hoc codes, mirror RFC 7807's problem+json shape inside `extensions`: a `type` URI identifying the error class, `title` for human-readable category, `detail` for instance-specific context, and `status` for HTTP consumers bridging to REST. Apollo Server's `formatError` hook is where you enforce this contract — inspect each `GraphQLError`, check its `originalError`, then stamp normalized extensions before serialization. This matters because GraphQL runs over HTTP *and* WebSocket (subscriptions); clients shouldn't need protocol-specific error-parsing branches. A shared shape enforced in `formatError` guarantees every error surface — resolvers, auth directives, DataLoader callbacks — emits the same structure. Treat missing `type` URIs as a contract violation. Errors without them are debugging black holes; extensions are a first-class API surface, not an afterthought.

bytes.topic: Choosing a Protocol — REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC decision framework
bytes.title: GraphQL's Place in the REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC Decision Framework
bytes.body: GraphQL wins when clients have divergent data needs. A mobile client fetching a lean `{ user { id name } }` and a dashboard running a complex nested query with fragments both hit the same endpoint — no versioning, no over-fetching. The schema acts as a contract: `type Query { user(id: ID!): User }` is self-documenting and introspectable via `__schema`. Where GraphQL loses: binary streaming (gRPC wins with Protobuf), simple CRUD with uniform payloads (REST is lighter), and N+1 query traps without DataLoader batching. Choose GraphQL when: (1) multiple clients consume the same API with different shape requirements, (2) rapid frontend iteration is a priority and BFF round-trips are costly, (3) you need real-time via `Subscription` types alongside queries. Avoid it for file uploads, highly cacheable public APIs (HTTP cache headers don't map cleanly to POST-based GraphQL), or teams unfamiliar with resolver depth and schema design costs. The schema-first workflow with tools like `graphql-codegen` generating typed hooks is the real forcing function — it makes GraphQL's overhead pay off at scale.

bytes.topic: Performance — payload size, compression, connection reuse, multiplexing
bytes.title: GraphQL Performance — Shrinking Payloads, Reusing Connections, and Multiplexing Subscriptions
bytes.body:
GraphQL's flexible selection sets can still balloon payloads when clients over-fetch or send large query strings repeatedly. Three levers address this directly.

Automatic Persisted Queries (APQ): Apollo Client hashes the query with SHA-256 and sends only the hash. On a cache miss, the server requests the full document once; every subsequent request transmits the hash alone — cutting wire size 70–90% for deeply nested queries.

Brotli compression: GraphQL responses are JSON — highly repetitive and compressible. Set `Accept-Encoding: br` on the client and enable Brotli on Apollo Server via `expressMiddleware` with a compression middleware. Brotli achieves 15–25% better ratios than gzip on typical JSON payloads.

Subscription transport and HTTP/2 multiplexing: WebSocket-based subscriptions (`graphql-ws`) open a dedicated TCP connection per client. Migrating to `graphql-sse` (Server-Sent Events) lets subscriptions ride existing HTTP/2 streams, enabling true multiplexing and eliminating per-subscription handshake overhead. Pair this with `keepalive: true` on your `HttpLink` fetch options to reuse connections across query/mutation traffic.
